agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Subscription billing platform managing lifecycle states (activation, renewal, cancellation) with payment validation, retry logic, tier-specific grace periods, and free trial constraints.","bounded_context":"Subscription Billing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Subscription","definition":"A recurring billing agreement between a customer and the platform, progressing through states: trial, active, pending-renewal, grace-period, cancelled."},{"term":"Payment Method","definition":"A stored instrument (card, bank account) used to collect subscription fees; has a validation status (valid/failed)."},{"term":"Billing Cycle","definition":"The recurring period (e.g., monthly, annual) that defines when payment is collected."},{"term":"Renewal","definition":"The automated process of charging the subscriber's payment method to extend the subscription for another billing cycle."},{"term":"Grace Period","definition":"A time window after failed renewals during which the subscription remains active before cancellation is enforced (Premium tier: 14 days)."},{"term":"Free Trial","definition":"A time-limited subscription that provides access without payment, constrained to one active instance per email address."},{"term":"Premium Tier","definition":"A subscription level that receives enhanced cancellation protection via a 14-day grace period instead of the standard 3-strike retry rule."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A subscription cannot be activated with a failed payment method on file.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. Activating a subscription with a known-bad payment method would immediately create an inconsistent system state: an 'active' subscription that cannot collect revenue. It guards against data corruption where the system believes it has a paying subscriber but has no viable way to collect. No business policy change should allow activating against a provably broken payment instrument.","testable_assertion":"Given a payment method with status 'failed', any attempt to transition a subscription to 'active' state MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Subscriptions auto-renew 7 days before the billing cycle ends to allow retry time.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 7-day lead time is a business policy decision balancing retry window against early charges. A VP could reasonably change this to 5 days or 10 days based on payment processor SLAs, failure rate data, or customer feedback. The system would not be in a corrupt state if this window changed — it is a tunable operational parameter.","testable_assertion":"Given an active subscription, the renewal process MUST be initiated exactly 7 days before the current billing cycle's end date."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"After 3 failed renewal attempts, the subscription is automatically cancelled.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 3-attempt threshold is a business policy balancing revenue recovery against cost of retries and customer experience. Management could change this to 2 or 5 attempts based on churn analysis. The system's data integrity is not compromised by a different retry count — this is a configurable policy, not a physical or logical constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given a non-Premium subscription with 3 consecutive failed renewal attempts, the system MUST transition the subscription to 'cancelled' state."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A cancelled subscription cannot be reactivated — customers must create a new subscription.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state transition integrity constraint. Allowing reactivation of cancelled subscriptions would break the state machine's directed graph, potentially resurrecting billing history, old pricing, expired promotions, or audit-trail integrity. The rule that customers must create a NEW subscription ensures clean accounting, fresh terms acceptance, and unambiguous audit trails. Violating this corrupts the subscription lifecycle model.","testable_assertion":"Given a subscription in 'cancelled' state, any attempt to transition it to 'active' state MUST be rejected. The only path to active service is creating a new Subscription entity."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium tier subscribers get a 14-day grace period before cancellation instead of the 3-strike rule.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 14-day grace period and its applicability to Premium tier are business policy decisions reflecting a differentiated service level. The duration (14 days) and the tier it applies to (Premium) are both tunable parameters. A product decision could extend this to 21 days or apply it to additional tiers. No system integrity is violated by changing these values.","testable_assertion":"Given a Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal, the system MUST NOT cancel the subscription until 14 calendar days have elapsed since the first failed renewal attempt, regardless of retry count."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Free trial subscriptions cannot have more than one active trial per email address at any time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency constraint preventing abuse of the trial system. If violated, a single user could run unlimited concurrent trials, which corrupts the business model's assumption that trials are a one-time acquisition tool. This is analogous to a uniqueness constraint — the system is in an invalid state if two active trials exist for the same email. Unlike a policy threshold that can be tuned, the concept of 'one active trial per identity' is a fundamental integrity rule for trial-based acquisition.","testable_assertion":"Given an email address with an existing active free trial subscription, any attempt to create a second free trial subscription for that email MUST be rejected."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer attempts to activate a subscription","when":"The payment method on file has a 'failed' status","then":"The system rejects the activation and returns an error indicating the payment method must be updated"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An active subscription with a billing cycle ending on date D","when":"The current date reaches D minus 7 days","then":"The system initiates the auto-renewal charge against the stored payment method"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A standard (non-Premium) subscription with 2 prior failed renewal attempts","when":"The 3rd renewal attempt fails","then":"The system automatically transitions the subscription to 'cancelled' state and notifies the customer"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A subscription in 'cancelled' state","when":"A customer or system attempts to reactivate it","then":"The system rejects the reactivation and directs the customer to create a new subscription"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal attempt","when":"Fewer than 14 calendar days have elapsed since the first failure","then":"The subscription remains active and the system continues retry attempts without cancelling"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal","when":"14 calendar days have elapsed since the first failure without a successful payment","then":"The system automatically cancels the subscription"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A customer with an active free trial subscription on email X","when":"An attempt is made to create another free trial for email X","then":"The system rejects the second trial and returns an error indicating an active trial already exists"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A customer whose previous free trial has ended (expired or cancelled)","when":"An attempt is made to create a new free trial for the same email","then":"The system's behavior is undefined by current rules — this is an edge case requiring clarification (see edge cases)"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Payment method fails AFTER activation but BEFORE first renewal","expected_behavior":"The subscription remains active until the renewal window. The failed-payment-method check is an activation gate, not a continuous enforcement. Renewal will naturally handle failures via the retry/cancellation logic."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber's payment method is updated during the 14-day grace period","expected_behavior":"The system should attempt an immediate retry with the new payment method. If successful, the grace period resets and the subscription continues."},{"scenario":"Customer has a cancelled free trial and attempts a new free trial with the same email","expected_behavior":"Rules only prohibit more than one ACTIVE trial. A new trial after a previous trial ended should be governed by a separate policy (e.g., 'one trial ever per email' vs 'one active trial'). Current spec says 'active trial' — so a new trial should be allowed unless an additional business rule exists."},{"scenario":"Renewal attempt #3 fails on a standard subscription that was upgraded to Premium mid-cycle","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous — does the Premium grace period apply retroactively to in-flight failures, or only to failures that begin after the upgrade? Needs clarification."},{"scenario":"A subscription is cancelled after 3 failures, but payment actually succeeded (late processor confirmation)","expected_behavior":"The cancellation is final per BR-4. The payment should be refunded. Customer must create a new subscription. This prevents state machine corruption from async payment confirmations."},{"scenario":"Free trial with no payment method on file attempts to convert to paid subscription","expected_behavior":"BR-1 applies — a valid (non-failed) payment method must be on file before the subscription can transition to active paid status."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"These rules directly protect revenue integrity. The renewal lead time (7 days) maximizes payment recovery. The 3-strike and grace period rules balance revenue recovery against customer goodwill. Trial abuse prevention protects the acquisition funnel's unit economics.","operational":"Auto-renewal and auto-cancellation reduce manual intervention. The no-reactivation rule simplifies the subscription state machine and audit trail. Grace periods for Premium create a tier differentiation that must be operationally supported.","user":"Standard subscribers face faster cancellation (3 strikes) which may feel aggressive. Premium subscribers get a better experience with 14-day grace. The no-reactivation rule creates friction for customers who want to return — they must re-enter payment details and may lose historical data or pricing.","risk":"The no-reactivation rule is the highest business risk — it increases churn friction but protects system integrity. Late payment confirmations after cancellation create refund exposure. Trial uniqueness enforcement depends on email as identity, which is spoofable (email+1@, aliases).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers (Standard tier)","impact":"Subject to 3-strike cancellation; must create new subscription if cancelled; friction on re-enrollment"},{"group":"Customers (Premium tier)","impact":"Protected by 14-day grace period; better retention experience during payment issues"},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"Predictable revenue recovery via retry logic; clean audit trails from no-reactivation rule; refund exposure from late payment confirmations"},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Must handle complaints from cancelled subscribers who cannot reactivate; need clear scripts for directing to new subscription creation"},{"group":"Product/Growth team","impact":"Trial abuse prevention protects acquisition metrics; one-trial-per-email may be too permissive (allows sequential trials)"}]},"confidence":{"level":72,"reasoning":"The spec is reasonably well-defined for the core rules. Confidence is below 80 because several edge cases remain unaddressed: the interaction between Premium grace period and the standard retry mechanism, the policy on sequential trials (vs concurrent), and the behavior during tier upgrades mid-failure. These are not contradictions — they are areas where the spec is silent.","high_confidence_areas":["Activation requires valid payment method","Cancelled subscriptions cannot be reactivated","One active trial per email uniqueness constraint","7-day renewal lead time"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Premium grace period replaces or supplements retry attempts","Policy on sequential free trials (same email, previous trial ended)","Behavior when tier changes during an active retry/grace period","Whether the 7-day renewal window means exactly day 7 or anytime within the last 7 days"],"assumptions":["Email address is the unique identity for trial enforcement","Premium tier grace period completely replaces the 3-strike rule rather than supplementing it","Failed payment method status is determined at activation time, not continuously enforced","The 3-strike rule applies to consecutive failures within a single renewal cycle, not cumulative across the subscription lifetime"]},"escalations":[],"rejection_reasons":[]}
